items.title: The Compounding Software Factory
items.summary: Luca and CircleCI CTO Rob Zuber argue AI-native teams can invert the typical engineering-degradation curve by pairing automated hygiene gates, ADR-captured decisions, and agent-generated maintenance automations into a self-improving feedback loop. Matters because it operationalises compound engineering with concrete tooling patterns rather than staying theoretical.

items.title: Long-Running Agents
items.summary: Addy Osmani's 26-minute guide explains how agents maintain progress across context windows and sandboxes, recover from failure, and leave resumable structural artifacts, covering what engineers can build today without writing orchestrators from scratch. Matters because it bridges theory and practice for persistent agent architecture.

items.title: The Moat or the Commons
items.summary: Argues that the trillion-dollar capex bet on frontier-model monopoly dynamics is breaking as open-weight models from Chinese labs commoditise capability at pace, forcing a policy question about whether governments subsidise the private moat or the open commons. Matters because it frames the structural crisis in frontier AI funding with economic precision.

items.title: Automated Alignment Researchers: Using LLMs to Scale Scalable Oversight
items.summary: Anthropic's Claude-powered Automated Alignment Researchers ran in parallel sandboxes and closed nearly the entire remaining performance gap on a week-long weak-to-strong supervision problem, outperforming human researchers at roughly 2 per agent-hour. Matters because it is the clearest demonstration yet that AI can meaningfully accelerate its own alignment research.

items.title: Symphony: An Open-Source Spec for Codex Orchestration
items.summary: OpenAI's Symphony turns Linear issue boards into autonomous Codex agent control planes, delivering a reported 5x increase in landed PRs by eliminating human supervision from the implementation loop. Matters because it operationalises manager-of-agents at the project level and shows how work-tracker infrastructure becomes the agentic coordination layer.

items.title: Compute Is the New Cash
items.summary: Dan Shipper interviews Stripe's head of data and AI to show that top AI companies reach �M ARR in 18 months (3× faster than SaaS), that fraud has evolved to target compute credits rather than payments, and that agents are beginning to act as economic participants. Matters because Stripe's payment data gives credible empirical weight to the services-as-software transition thesis.

items.title: One App to Rule All Knowledge Work
items.summary: Every's head of growth describes routing 80% of daily knowledge work through Codex — email triage, KPI tracking, recruiting — using agent-designed automations and a compound knowledge plugin. Matters as a practitioner account of living-software inside a small company, with specific workflow details that go beyond theory.

items.title: Agent Memory Patterns
items.summary: Classifies the three mutable memory types agents use — Files, Memory Blocks, and Skills — and explains how editable memory transforms agent power, covering practical patterns for adding persistent state without custom orchestrators. Matters as a clean taxonomy engineers can apply immediately when building agents with longer horizons.

items.title: Can Agents Replace the Search Stack?
items.summary: Empirically shows that iterative agentic search yields significantly better answers than single-shot retrieval, but that training-data boundaries and what-the-model-doesn't-know remain hard ceilings no amount of tool use overcomes. Matters because it gives grounded empirical limits to the agent-native-browsing thesis.

items.title: GPT-5.5: The System Card
items.summary: Zvi's analysis finds GPT-5.5 competitive with Claude Opus on factual queries and web search but weaker on open-ended reasoning, with alignment similar to prior models and no new major risks flagged. Matters as a comparative capability and safety benchmark from the field's most systematic system-card analyst.

items.title: To Train or Not To Train
items.summary: Argues that product companies integrating into the model layer should do post-training where frontier models underserve them — citing Intercom Fin Apex running at 1/5 frontier cost with higher resolution rates — not pre-training from scratch. Matters because it gives an actionable decision framework for the build-vs-buy question in a post-commoditisation era.

items.title: The Task Is Not the Job
items.summary: Rebuts Amodei's job-displacement thesis by arguing that roles are bundles of shifting tasks in organisational contexts, so automating discrete tasks does not eliminate entry-level positions when firms reorganise around agent output. Matters because it grounds the jobs debate in labour economics rather than capability speculation.

items.title: AI Rewards Strict APIs
items.summary: Argues that AI agents struggle with ambiguous APIs, making strict, well-specified API design a competitive moat for services that want to be reliably orchestrated by LLMs. Matters because it gives engineers a concrete design principle — specificity over flexibility — for building AI-interoperable software.

items.title: Laguna XS.2 and M.1: A Deeper Dive
items.summary: Poolside introduces Laguna M.1 (foundation) and XS.2 (small open-weight, Apache 2.0) as agentic coding models explicitly designed for long-horizon work, available free on OpenRouter. Matters as a credible open-weight challenger to proprietary coding agents that brings long-horizon design intent, not just benchmark scores.

items.title: Experts Have World Models. LLMs Have Word Models.
items.summary: Draws a sharp line between LLM statistical token prediction and true causal world models, showing LLMs are systematically vulnerable in adversarial multiagent scenarios requiring theory of mind or hidden-information reasoning. Matters because it identifies a precise capability gap between current agents and what robust agentic behavior actually requires.

items.title: Headless Everything for Personal AI
items.summary: Simon Willison argues AI agents perform reliably against headless APIs but degrade on GUI-based tools, citing Salesforce exposing its entire Agentforce and Slack platforms as APIs/MCP/CLI as the right architectural pattern. Matters as a practitioner call for headless design as an infrastructure prerequisite for agentic operation.

items.title: OpenAI's Q4 2026 IPO Might Not Happen
items.summary: Documents OpenAI's CFO being excluded from key infrastructure financial discussions while the company misses revenue and user targets, making the compressed Q4 IPO timeline implausible. Matters because it surfaces the structural tension between OpenAI's capex obligations and its revenue trajectory at a pivotal moment.

items.title: Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
items.summary: Google researchers derive the first quantitative scaling principles for agent systems across 180 configurations, showing that adding more agents hits a ceiling and can degrade performance if not matched to task structure. Matters because it brings rigorous empirical grounding to multi-agent system design.

items.title: Scaling without Slop
items.summary: swyx argues the next scaling frontier is output quality rather than parameter count, with inference-time compute and RL-tuned reasoning becoming the dominant levers as pretraining yields diminish. Matters as a clear articulation of the post-pretraining scaling thesis from the AI Engineer community.

items.title: How ChatGPT Serves Ads
items.summary: Reverse-engineers ChatGPT's ad attribution loop, from Fernet-encrypted click tokens injected into conversation to a browser-side SDK reporting product views back to OpenAI's backend. Matters because it shows AI assistants are building a parallel ad infrastructure at the model layer that could reshape digital commerce.

items.title: Former Google DeepMind Researcher Raises Record .1B Seed Funding to Pursue Superintelligence
items.summary: David Silver (DeepMind RL lead) raises a record .1B seed round for Ineffable Intelligence with NVIDIA and Google backing, pursuing superintelligence research. Matters as a signal of continued conviction capital flowing into AGI timelines despite broader AI financial strain.

items.title: DeepSeek Cuts V4-Pro Prices by 75%
items.summary: DeepSeek slashes V4-Pro model pricing by 75% and reduces cache hit costs by 90%, continuing deflationary pressure on AI model pricing from Chinese labs. Matters as a concrete data point in the scaling-cost debate and the moat-erosion thesis.

items.title: Karpathy: Notes on 80% Agent-Driven Coding Workflow
items.summary: Karpathy documents going from 20% to 80% agent-driven coding in weeks, sharing detailed notes on workflow changes, model trust, and remaining rough edges of agent-native development. Matters because his practitioner credibility makes this a definitive benchmark for vibe-coding adoption among top researchers.

items.title: Batch API Is Terrible for One Agent. It Might Be Great for a Fleet.
items.summary: Shows the Batch API 50% discount is economically unviable for single-agent tasks due to latency, but highly attractive when agents can be pooled and routed through intelligent proxies. Matters as practical economics for engineers designing multi-agent fleet architectures.

items.title: An Interview with OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and AWS CEO Matt Garman about Bedrock Managed Agents
items.summary: Ben Thompson's 50-minute deep dive into how AWS and OpenAI are structuring Bedrock Managed Agents, what the Microsoft exclusivity amendment means for AI distribution, and how removing the AGI clause reshapes the OpenAI-Microsoft power dynamic. Matters because it lays out the emerging two-cloud world where agent orchestration infrastructure—not raw model access—becomes the competitive moat.

items.title: DeepSeek V4—almost on the frontier, a fraction of the price
items.summary: Willison analyzes DeepSeek V4 as near-frontier capability at dramatically lower cost, continuing the pattern where open-weight models compress the return on closed-model frontier investment. The piece matters as a practitioner-calibrated read on what the closing open/closed capability gap means for developers and the gatekeeping-vs-open debate.

items.title: The people do not yearn for automation
items.summary: Willison argues that AI automation proponents underestimate a core demand-side constraint: most people do not want to hand off their tasks to AI, and the gap between what AI can automate and what people actually want automated is a serious, underappreciated brake on adoption. A strong practitioner counterweight to the services-as-software and autonomous-agent optimism.

items.title: The Zig project's rationale for their firm anti-AI contribution policy
items.summary: Willison documents Zig's principled ban on LLM-generated code contributions, one of the most stringent anti-LLM policies in open source. Relevant as a concrete counterpoint to blanket vibe-coding adoption and to the gatekeeping-vs-open debate in software communities.

items.title: Shopify's AI Phase Transition: 2026 Usage Explosion, Unlimited Opus-4.6 Token Budget, Tangle, Tangent, SimGym
items.summary: Shopify CTO Mikhail Parakhin reveals that 100% of the company's workforce uses AI daily after a late-2025 token-consumption inflection; the episode covers Shopify's proprietary agent platforms (Tangle, Tangent, SimGym) and the shift to critique-driven PR review at scale. Essential case study of a 00B company achieving AI-native org design in practice.

items.title: Notion's Token Town: 5 Rebuilds, 100+ Tools, MCP vs CLIs and the Software Factory Future
items.summary: Simon Last and Sarah Sachs of Notion describe five architectural rebuilds of their Custom Agents system, practical tradeoffs between MCP and CLIs for agent integration, and Notion's vision of software factories made of agents that spec, code, test, and maintain codebases. Strong practitioner case study of a productivity platform going agent-native.

items.title: The Last Software Engineer
items.summary: Kent C. Dodds asks what remains valuable for software engineers as AI agents take over implementation: his answer is judgment about what to build and product taste, not coding skill. Reframes the "engineers are obsolete" narrative toward what remains durable when vibe-coding handles execution.

items.title: Opus 4.7's New Tokenizer: What It Actually Costs
items.summary: OpenRouter's analysis shows Claude Opus 4.7's improved tokenizer raises actual costs 12–27% despite unchanged sticker prices, with the exception of short prompts that become cheaper. Important for practitioners running agent fleets who need to recalibrate cost models when switching to the new tokenizer.

items.title: The Recurrent Transformer: Greater Effective Depth and Efficient Decoding
items.summary: Introduces a novel architecture that adds layerwise recurrence to Transformers, enabling complex iterative reasoning without massive layer stacks via persistent key-value pairs. Relevant to the world-models and scaling-laws-revisionism debate about whether inference-time architectural innovation can substitute for brute-force parameter scaling.

items.title: AI Evals Are Becoming the New Compute Bottleneck
items.summary: Documents how AI evaluation costs have escalated to rival training costs, with some benchmark runs reaching tens of thousands of dollars and creating unequal access to external validation. Matters because eval bottlenecks will increasingly determine which labs and teams can iterate fastest on capability improvements.

items.title: Lessons on Building MCP Servers
items.summary: Practitioner guide arguing that effective MCP toolchains require making the next tool call blindingly obvious at each step, since models scan context and grab whatever looks most probable rather than planning sequences. Essential grounding for teams building agent pipelines on top of MCP.

items.title: Mistral Medium 3.5 Powers Remote Vibe Agents for Long Async Coding Tasks
items.summary: Mistral releases Medium 3.5 (128B dense) paired with Vibe remote agents that run long asynchronous coding tasks in the cloud via CLI or Le Chat, scoring high on SWE-Bench Verified. Represents another major lab shipping a frontier agentic coding workflow on a dense rather than MoE architecture.

items.title: LaDiR: Latent Diffusion Enhances LLMs for Text Reasoning
items.summary: Introduces the Latent Diffusion Reasoner, combining continuous latent representation with iterative diffusion so LLMs can plan and revise reasoning holistically in parallel. Outperforms autoregressive and diffusion baselines on accuracy, diversity, and interpretability.

items.title: Darwinian Specialization in AI: Why the Inference Market Is Fragmenting
items.summary: Argues the inference market is diverging into latency-tier, multimodal, and edge segments with distinct serving requirements, creating room for multiple infrastructure winners rather than a winner-takes-all GPU cloud. Applies Darwinian framing to explain why specialized models and infra beat general-purpose deployments.

items.title: Build Programmatic Agents with the Cursor SDK
items.summary: Cursor releases a public TypeScript SDK giving developers access to the same runtime, harness, and frontier models that power the Cursor IDE, usable locally or on Cursor cloud. Marks a shift from IDE-only agent tooling toward an extensible programmatic agent substrate.

items.title: The $112 Billion Quarter: Hyperscalers Bet the Farm on AI
items.summary: Google, Amazon, and Microsoft collectively spent $112B in Q1 2026 capex with Google now outspending Microsoft, and BofA forecasts $175B in hyperscaler debt issuance for the year. Central thesis: whichever hyperscaler owns the model layer will compound fastest.

items.title: Agents Can Now Create Cloudflare Accounts, Buy Domains, and Deploy
items.summary: Cloudflare announces end-to-end agentic deployment: agents can create accounts, purchase domains, and ship to production without human intervention except optional approval gates at ToS steps. A concrete production milestone for autonomous agents as economic actors.

items.title: 2026: The Year of Churn
items.summary: Argues AI filling 60% of niche SaaS functionality is now sufficient for buyers to cancel $80K contracts, and annual deal structures have been masking how much churn is already occurring. Signals-based view of SaaS revenue erosion in real time.

items.title: Locked, Stocked, and Losing Budget: AI Vendor Lock-in Bites Back
items.summary: Documents how enterprises discover token-based AI billing creates unpredictable costs while switching costs keep them trapped, forecasting that fixed-price SaaS tiers will give way to consumption pricing across the stack. Exposes the gap between vendor promises and actual procurement reality.

items.title: Direction Over Speed
items.summary: Argues AI broke the old cover of shipping-as-productivity because prototypes are cheap and specs write themselves, making knowing why to build something the true bottleneck. Calls for smaller PM-closer-to-the-work teams where the model handles execution but humans own direction.

items.title: Who Isn't Using GPT 5.5
items.summary: Every.to examines why practitioners with mature Claude and Codex agent workflows are not switching to GPT-5.5 despite speed advantages, finding workflow lock-in and skill integration are larger barriers than capability gaps. Ground-level adoption data on model switching costs in 2026.

items.title: Sequoia AI Ascent 2026: Karpathy on Software 3.0 and Agentic Engineering
items.summary: Karpathy argues Software 3.0 (prompting as programming) is the dominant new paradigm, with vibe coding raising the floor and agentic engineering raising the ceiling, and human understanding remaining the ultimate bottleneck. One of the clearest frameworks from a leading AI researcher on the transition to professional agentic work.

items.title: The Folder Is the Agent
items.summary: Kieran Klaassen describes running 44 specialized AI agents by pointing Opus at domain-specific folders, turning a generalist model into a Rails engineer, ops specialist, or product analyst through context alone. The thesis: folder structure has become the primary interface for agent specialization.

items.title: The 50th Percentile Rule for Token Use
items.summary: Documents Chainguard's policy that engineering managers must sit at the 50th percentile of token usage among their reports: too few means lacking operational context, too many means not delegating to the team. A concrete org-design mechanism for embedding AI fluency into engineering leadership.

items.title: AIE Europe Debrief + Agent Labs Thesis (Latent Space x Unsupervised Learning)
items.summary: Latent Space debrief of AI Engineer Europe alongside a thesis for Agent Labs as an emerging organizational category: dedicated teams within companies that build, evaluate, and deploy autonomous agent systems. Maps the technical and org patterns at the frontier of enterprise agent deployment.

items.title: Firefox Maker Torches Google for Building Prompt API into Browser
items.summary: Mozilla argues that Google's Prompt API lets web pages query a browser-embedded LLM, creating severe interoperability and neutrality problems for the open web. Raises the governance question of whether AI inference belongs in the browser platform layer.

items.title: Elon Musk Testifies That xAI Trained Grok on OpenAI Models
items.summary: Musk's court testimony confirms xAI used distillation from OpenAI models to train Grok, validating the widely-held assumption that major labs routinely distill each other's outputs to avoid falling behind. A rare primary-source confirmation of a practice central to understanding how closed frontier models propagate.

items.title: Granite 4.1 LLMs: How They're Built
items.summary: IBM details a five-phase pre-training approach for Granite 4.1 (3B-30B) on 15T tokens where multi-stage RL lets the 8B model match the previous 32B MoE on enterprise benchmarks. Demonstrates how targeted RL pipelines are closing the gap between dense and mixture-of-experts architectures.

items.title: DataPRM: Reliable Data Analysis Agents via Environment-Aware Process Reward Models
items.summary: Introduces DataPRM, a process reward model that detects silent errors in data analysis agent execution and improves downstream performance across benchmarks. Addresses a key reliability gap for deploying autonomous data agents in production.

items.title: xAI Has Launched Grok 4.3
items.summary: xAI launches Grok 4.3, improving cost-per-intelligence over its predecessor — higher scores on the Intelligence Index at lower per-run cost. Marks a step in commodity frontier model competition where price/capability ratio becomes the key differentiator.

items.title: Claude Security is Now in Public Beta
items.summary: Anthropic opens Claude Security to public beta for Enterprise customers, using Opus 4.7 to identify and patch software vulnerabilities integrated with Microsoft Security and Palo Alto Networks. Demonstrates agents moving from code generation into active security operations.

items.title: xAI's Move to Buy Cursor Shows the Promise & the Risks for AI Applications Startups
items.summary: Deep analysis of the Cursor/xAI 0B deal: Cursor has explosive revenue but unsustainable compute costs relying on Anthropic and OpenAI models; xAI has Colossus supercomputer but no distribution. Argues the deal exposes the core tension for AI application companies — proprietary models vs. product — and sets a template for vertical integration plays.

items.title: KV Cache Locality: The Hidden Variable in Your LLM Serving Cost
items.summary: Explains how KV cache locality acts as a hidden throughput multiplier: the same hardware serving the same traffic produces measurably different performance depending on whether load balancers understand token locality. Matters for anyone optimizing inference infrastructure at scale.

items.title: Where the Goblins Came From
items.summary: OpenAI traces how a retired 'Nerdy' personality reward signal caused goblin metaphors to proliferate 175% across GPT-5.1 — a vivid case study in how RL reward signals leak across training contexts. Illustrates the difficulty of keeping learned behaviors scoped to intended conditions.

items.title: Continually Improving Our Agent Harness
items.summary: Cursor explains how they continuously tune their agent harness using vision-driven development, A/B testing, and dynamic context adaptation — treating the harness itself as the product layer above the model. Key insight: harness engineering, not model selection, is now the main differentiator.

items.title: What You're Actually Writing When You Write a skill.md
items.summary: Explains that a skill.md is not documentation but a runtime artifact — a structured prompt that shapes agent behavior by controlling what the agent sees at inference time. Understanding the runtime changes everything about how you write at the surface.

items.title: Speculative Decoding for RL Training
items.summary: Applies speculative decoding to RL rollouts without changing output distributions, delivering up to 1.8x throughput gains and projected 2.5x end-to-end speedups at scale. Accelerates reasoning-RL training, reducing the compute cost of iterative self-improvement.

items.title: Qwen-Scope: Decoding Intelligence, Unleashing Potential
items.summary: Qwen-Scope is an interpretability toolkit for the Qwen3/3.5 model series that exposes internal mechanisms for controllable inference, model optimization, and evaluation distribution analysis. Open interpretability tooling moving from research labs into production model ecosystems.

items.title: GitHub Is Sinking
items.summary: Developer essay arguing that Copilot is effectively DDoS-ing GitHub with low-quality AI-generated PRs and issues — flooding the platform with 'slop' that degrades the signal-to-noise ratio for maintainers. Practitioner skepticism about AI code quality at scale.

items.title: Silicon Valley Is Bracing for a Permanent Underclass
items.summary: NYT Opinion essay based on three months of interviews with researchers, economists, and frontier lab employees: the consensus inside AI is that ordinary workers will lose economic leverage, but an AI underclass is a societal choice not an inevitability. Argues for policy intervention before the window closes.

items.title: The Inference Economy: Token Use
items.summary: Argues token demand is structurally rising while per-token cost improvements are slowing — meaning businesses that blindly throw more LLM compute at problems will face sharply rising costs. Frames token management (right-sized model, reasoning only when needed, provider flexibility) as a core competency for AI-native companies.

items.title: 7 Attributes of AI-Native Startups
items.summary: Identifies seven structural characteristics of AI-native startups, centering data flywheels as the primary competitive advantage. Useful taxonomy for understanding how AI-first companies differ from traditional software companies in org design and growth mechanics.

items.title: Claude Code for Product Managers
items.summary: Practitioner essay by Marcus Moretti on running an entire product with Claude Code as a solo general manager — from planning and roadmaps to tickets and research, all in a single chat thread. Argues the bottleneck shifts from execution to judgment: the only document he wrote was the roadmap vision.

items.title: One Developer, Two Dozen Agents, Zero Alignment
items.summary: Maggie Appleton (GitHub Next) argues all coding agents are single-player experiences but software development is not — alignment (shared context, intent, decisions) has always been the bottleneck, and agents make the cost of misalignment exponentially higher. Introduces a research prototype for collaborative AI engineering.

items.title: Every Bubble Looks Different
items.summary: Argues that every market cycle invents a new name for the same fantasy — software that generates returns passively — and that AI is repeating this pattern. A concise bubble-skeptic counter-narrative to the "this time is different" AI exceptionalism thesis.

items.title: Silicon Valley Is Bracing for a Permanent Underclass
items.summary: Long-form investigation into Silicon Valley insiders who believe AI will displace millions of jobs and concentrate power in the hands of AI companies and capital owners, creating a permanent underclass of economically irrelevant workers. A foundational piece for the bubble-skeptics and post-AGI-skeptics schools: the concern is not that AI fails but that it succeeds in ways that are catastrophically inequitable.

items.title: Approaching Zero Bugs?
items.summary: Proposes a measurable signal for whether AI coding tools are truly improving: track the age distribution of reported and fixed bugs — once AI reaches escape velocity, only recently-introduced bugs should survive. A practitioner's lens on compound-engineering progress from the maintainer of curl.

items.title: How AI Efficiency Is Subtly Disrupting the Interactions That Build Strong Teams
items.summary: Research from MIT, Google, and Harvard shows that AI tools are eliminating informal colleague interactions ("bugging") that build the trust, psychological safety, and innovation critical to high-performing teams — trading visible efficiency for invisible resilience. Argues leaders must actively design for serendipitous human interaction as a counter-force to AI-driven isolation.

items.title: The Forgotten Conversation Problem in AI Chat
items.summary: AI chat platforms inherited a messaging-app architecture for what is actually knowledge work, leaving past insights unrecoverable because they lack keyword search, atomic linking, and user-controlled knowledge architecture. Decades of research (from Vannevar Bush to modern PKM) show the solution already exists — the AI industry is just applying it too slowly.

items.title: Claude Code for Product Managers
items.summary: Practitioner essay on how one general manager runs a full product solo using AI for planning, monitoring, and research — detailing the specific workflows, Claude Code invocations, and judgment calls required. A concrete implementation guide for the manager-of-agents thesis in a PM context.

items.title: Sign of the Future: GPT-5.5
items.summary: Mollick argues GPT-5.5 with GPT-Image-2 in Codex signals a shift where the bottleneck in AI-assisted development is no longer "can you make the first version" but "can you tell what's off" — taste and evaluation judgment become the scarce resource. A practitioner essay from a key voice in the extrapolators school on what frontier capability gains actually change in daily work.

items.title: Personal leverage is now unlimited
items.summary: Luca Rossi argues that solo engineers now have unlimited personal leverage if they combine tech skills, taste, and drive—illustrated by the trajectory from Copilot to Cursor to Claude Code, the latter built by just two engineers. The piece makes the case that AI has removed the ceiling on what one person can ship, not the floor, and that taste and domain judgment are the remaining scarce resources.

items.title: Every Engineer Is a Manager Now — with Chris Lattner
items.summary: Chris Lattner (creator of LLVM, Swift, founder of Modular) argues that AI is forcing engineers to act like managers—directing agents, reviewing output, and setting direction instead of writing code—and explains why Modular is actively hiring new-grad engineers who bring AI-native intuition. The interview surfaces a counter-narrative to the all-senior-team orthodoxy: good teams need multiple experience levels learning from each other.

items.title: A Guide to Agent-native Product Management
items.summary: Marcus Moretti walks through compound engineering slash-commands for PMs—/ce:strategy and /ce:product-pulse—where agents replace analytics dashboards with briefings that accumulate in a folder as the product's persistent memory. The core argument is that 'the conversation is the work' and that PM value shifts entirely to strategic framing and judgment as implementation and analysis become agent tasks.

items.title: We need RSS for sharing abundant vibe-coded apps
items.summary: Simon Willison argues that AI-accelerated app development makes shipping a micro-app feel more like posting on a blog than launching a product, and proposes RSS as the distribution layer for the coming flood of personal tools. The piece surfaces a concrete infrastructure gap: there is no discovery mechanism for the abundant, ephemeral, personal software that vibe-coding is producing.

items.title: Our evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-5.5 cyber capabilities
items.summary: The UK AI Safety Institute finds GPT-5.5 achieves a 71% pass rate on expert-level cyber tasks—comparable to Anthropic's Claude Mythos but generally available—and solved a complex reverse-engineering challenge autonomously in under 11 minutes for .73 in API costs. This is a landmark evaluation demonstrating that frontier models can independently conduct serious vulnerability research at scale, with significant implications for AI governance and safety.

items.title: Vulnerability Research Is Cooked
items.summary: Thomas Ptacek argues that frontier models will fundamentally disrupt vulnerability research within months—agents can find zero-days by pointing at a source tree because LLMs excel at exactly the implicit pattern-matching and constraint-solving that exploit development requires. The piece is alarming for its specificity: it cites early empirical results and warns that AI-driven security research will inevitably draw new and potentially harmful regulatory attention.

items.title: Dreamer: the Personal Agent OS — David Singleton
items.summary: David Singleton (ex-Stripe) and Hugo Barra present Dreamer, a consumer platform where a "Sidekick" agent builds and customizes other agents via natural language—positioning it as the personal agent OS complete with its own SDK, logging, database, and prompt management. The episode was recorded just before the team announced they were joining Meta Superintelligence Labs, making it a sharp snapshot of what ambitious consumer-agent architecture looked like at the threshold.

items.title: An AI state of the union: We've passed the inflection point, dark factories are coming, and automation timelines
items.summary: Simon Willison marks November 2025 as the inflection point when AI coding agents crossed from "mostly works" to "actually works," and predicts 50% of engineers will be writing 95% AI code by end of 2026. The episode covers the dark-factory pattern, vibe coding vs. agentic engineering, and where workflow bottlenecks have now shifted—making it the most comprehensive practitioner state-of-the-union on AI engineering available.

items.title: How I Use AI to Code
items.summary: A thorough, opinionated guide to AI-assisted coding updated for April 2026 that argues anyone still tied to their IDE is working a year behind—and cites MIT research showing teams shipping 20% slower despite feeling faster. The guide covers mindset shifts, tool selection, and practical loop-frequency discipline, making it a grounded counterweight to pure vibe-coding enthusiasm.

items.title: ICLR 2026: 12 papers on making AI systems reliable, efficient, and secure
items.summary: Lambda rounds up twelve standout ICLR 2026 papers covering long-horizon agentic planning under sparse rewards, alignment with safety constraints, structured world modeling, and inference-time efficiency. Useful as a research signal for which technical directions the academic community is betting on for reliable, safe agent systems.

items.title: Anthropic Tests Jupiter-v1-p Ahead of Its Developer Conference
items.summary: Anthropic is red-teaming a new internal build codenamed Jupiter-v1-p before the Code with Claude developer conference on May 6, consistent with its responsible scaling policy requiring jailbreak probes and constitutional classifier stress tests. The timing strongly suggests a frontier-class model announcement is imminent, with safety hardening the gating step.

items.title: Designing, Refining, and Maintaining Agent Skills at Perplexity
items.summary: Perplexity details how it builds modular Agent Skills for its frontier products: hierarchical design, real-query-driven necessity tests, multi-model evaluation, and constant iteration. Each Skill carries an inherent cost, so simplicity and efficiency are primary constraints—this is the most detailed practitioner breakdown of agentic skill architecture published to date.

items.title: Synthetic Computer Environments for Agent Training
items.summary: A scalable method generates realistic virtual computer environments and long-horizon simulations, producing rich training signals that improve agent performance on productivity tasks without manual annotation. This approach addresses the key bottleneck in agentic RL: obtaining diverse, grounded supervision at scale.

items.title: You Are Not Immune to Mode Collapse
items.summary: Mode collapse—AI systems repeatedly generating the most statistically common outputs—is examined as a structural failure mode affecting models, grant-making, and creative domains alike. The post argues that without deliberate variability injection, optimization toward past successes produces homogeneous and brittle outputs.

items.title: Clouded Judgement 5.1.26 — The Death of Per-Seat Pricing?
items.summary: Microsoft is replacing seat-based SaaS licensing with a hybrid model where seats become packaging for prepaid token consumption, with overage charged per agent action or per outcome. The analysis argues this is a structural shift that will force every SaaS company to redesign its revenue model around usage rather than headcount.

items.title: After AI-First Comes AI-Only
items.summary: Schreiber distinguishes AI-first (AI as pilot assistance) from AI-only (end-to-end machine execution where humans only set goals, constraints, and escalation conditions). This framing repositions humans from workflow participants to system stewards—a clear articulation of the services-as-software endgame.

items.title: Agent Skills
items.summary: AI coding agents skip senior-engineering discipline unless it is encoded as a Skill—a structured capability the agent cannot talk itself out of. Osmani argues that encoding review, testing, and documentation as inescapable agent routines is now the primary job of the senior engineer.

items.title: Sometimes Smart Planners Lose to Simple Markets
items.summary: Multi-agent orchestration is not analogous to human organizations: if any subtask is wrong, individually competent agents still produce wrong answers, while markets succeed by surfacing local information that cannot be centrally shared. The post challenges hub-and-spoke agent architecture and the analogy between AI hubs and human managers.

items.title: How Might a Browser Be Developed?
items.summary: Explores how browser architecture and web development conventions will need to change as LLMs become capable agents navigating and interacting with the web. Argues that the browser itself is due for an LLM-native redesign rather than bolted-on AI extensions.

items.title: Prompt Engineering Is Permanent
items.summary: Prompt engineering in 2026 is not about clever phrasing but about making AI systems reliable when they have context, tools, memory, permissions, and real consequences—it has become a permanent engineering discipline for production agentic systems. The post rebuts the idea that better models will eliminate the need for careful prompt design.

items.title: Specsmaxxing
items.summary: Spec-writing—not code-writing—is argued to be where software engineering actually happens in the AI-assisted era, with detailed YAML-based feature specifications as the primary artifact engineers produce for agents. The post examines the upside of spec-driven development and the psychosis risk of over-speccing.

items.title: Codex Goes to Work
items.summary: Every covers how one-person teams are running full product cycles with agents as teammates: compound engineering Skills for strategy and product pulse, AI cost discipline frameworks, and medicine advancing at software speed. Agent-native product management is treated as a present-day practice, not a future aspiration.

items.title: How Did Large Language Models Get That Way? The Role of Transformers and Pretraining in GPT
items.summary: The first in a series on LLM architecture and its implications for reasoning, explaining why the transformer architecture was so impactful and how pretraining shaped the capabilities of modern GPT-style models. Situates LLMs as the largest computing infrastructure project in history.

items.title: How I Run the Tolaria Project
items.summary: Luca Rossi documents the agentic and human workflows he built to manage 200+ issues, 150 PRs, and 150+ feature requests for the Tolaria open-source project while working only part-time. Demonstrates how compound engineering with AI-assisted triage, backlog management, and release pipelines lets a solo developer sustain a fast-growing project at near-team velocity.

items.title: The Dawn of Codex-native Apps
items.summary: The AI workflow landscape is bifurcating: one track delegates to fully-autonomous agents (Codex-style), the other pairs a human with a collaborative agent sitting beside them during live work. The piece argues Codex-native app development is a distinct programming paradigm with its own design constraints, not just a feature of existing tools.

items.title: I Let ChatGPT Manage My Workweek
items.summary: The author configures ChatGPT as a personal AI project manager with real access to OKRs, calendar, Notion, and Slack, and evaluates whether it can drive week-to-week execution. A practitioner test of the manager-of-agents thesis applied to individual knowledge work, including honest accounting of where AI oversight breaks down.

items.title: Replit's Amjad Masad on the Cursor Deal, Fighting Apple, and Why He'd Rather Not Sell
items.summary: Replit is nearing a B run rate with 300% net revenue retention and positive gross margins, contrasting with Cursor's negative-margin model; Masad argues Replit's end-to-end agentic platform for non-technical users is the more durable position. A key data point in the vibe-coding and AI rebundling debate: which layer of the stack captures lasting margin.

items.title: How LLM Inference Works
items.summary: A clear walkthrough of the full inference pipeline—tokenization, embeddings, stacked self-attention—splitting generation into compute-bound prefill and memory-bound autoregressive decode on the same GPU. Essential background for engineers designing agent harnesses or optimizing inference cost.

items.title: Everyone Wants to Be AI-Pilled. Most Companies Are Still Level 1.
items.summary: Ann Miura-Ko adapts the AV autonomy framework to AI adoption, mapping most self-described AI-forward organizations to L1–L2 (personal productivity or functional silos), with L3+ requiring cross-tool agents acting through MCP and non-engineers shipping production internal tools. Sets a concrete benchmark for founders and operators measuring their actual vs. claimed AI maturity.

items.title: Anthropic is Working on Orbit, Its Upcoming Proactive Assistant
items.summary: Orbit is a briefing-and-insights system inside Claude and Claude Code that surfaces personalized, actionable insights drawn from connected work tools—proactively pushing information rather than waiting for user queries. Signals Anthropic's move toward always-on agentic assistants that monitor context in the background, a meaningful step toward autonomous agent architecture.

items.title: Import AI #455: Automating AI Research
items.summary: Jack Clark surveys the state of end-to-end AI-automated R&D—coding, experiment execution, long-horizon task autonomy—and puts a ~60% probability on self-improving AI systems by 2028, projecting a capital-heavy, human-light machine economy. Makes the case that AI research automation is already practical, compressing timelines for alignment and capability work alike.

items.title: Model-Harness-Fit
items.summary: Bustamante uses Terminal-Bench 2.0 data to show that frontier labs post-train models against their own harnesses—baking tool names, memory rituals, and system-prompt structures into the weights—such that a harness mismatch costs measurable reasoning tokens and real benchmark points. The thesis: harness selection is now a first-class engineering decision, not a configuration detail, and model-harness-fit rivals raw capability as a performance driver.

items.title: GPT-5.5 Price Increase: What It Actually Costs
items.summary: GPT-5.5 launched with a nominal 2x sticker price increase, but after accounting for the model generating fewer completion tokens on longer prompts, the real cost increase is 49–92% depending on workload. Practical benchmark for teams weighing whether to upgrade or route to cheaper frontier alternatives.

items.title: White House Considers Vetting AI Models Before They Are Released
items.summary: The Trump administration is discussing an executive order creating an AI working group with NSA and ODNI oversight of model releases, triggered in part by Anthropic's Mythos model disclosures—a reversal from the January 2025 revocation of Biden's safety order. Marks a potential governance inflection point: if enacted, pre-release vetting would directly constrain frontier model deployment timelines.

items.title: Anthropic and OpenAI Are Both Launching Joint Ventures for Enterprise AI Services
items.summary: Anthropic (.5B) and OpenAI (targeting 0B) both announced enterprise AI joint ventures backed by major financial firms, moving from model API providers toward full-stack AI service operators. Emblematic of the services-as-software transition: the most valuable labs are now selling outcomes and operations, not tokens.

items.title: Automated Weak-to-Strong Researcher
items.summary: Anthropic demonstrates AI agents that autonomously propose ideas, run experiments, and iterate on alignment research problems, with agents outperforming human researchers on key sub-tasks. The first published evidence that AI-accelerated alignment research is already practical, carrying significant implications for recursive self-improvement timelines.

items.title: What's Working Right Now in AI Search
items.summary: Buyers now arrive at product pages with AI-generated vendor comparisons already complete; one company doubled lead quality by editing pages LLMs already cited rather than chasing new citations, while another found AI answers describing their product incorrectly due to content drift. A practitioner guide to the emerging LLM-SEO landscape and its implication that AI systems now shape purchasing decisions before human intent is even expressed.

items.title: Computer Use Is 45x More Expensive Than Structured APIs
items.summary: Vision agents that screenshot web apps to extract data cost 45x more per task than structured API alternatives, because better models reduce error rates but not the number of screenshots required. This creates a strong economic case for investing in proper API surfaces (MCP, REST) rather than defaulting to LLM-driven computer use.

items.title: The Long Becoming: AI-Native vs AI-Enabled Companies
items.summary: The gap between AI-enabled and AI-native companies will mirror the cloud-enabled vs cloud-native divergence, where paths look identical initially then separate dramatically as compounding sets in. The sequence of bottlenecks is adoption → engineering velocity → product taste, with one CTO reporting 5x productivity in the top decile and taste emerging as the final rate limiter.

items.title: Services: The New Software
items.summary: The next trillion-dollar company will sell the work rather than the tool, attacking the $6-of-services-per-$1-of-software ratio that AI makes newly capturable by startups. The winning playbook: do the work by hand until the work tells you what to automate, building companies that look like services firms but earn like software.

items.title: The Context Window Has Been Shattered: Subquadratic Debuts a 12-Million-Token Window
items.summary: Subquadratic launched a model with a 12-million-token context window that outperforms GPT-5.5 on retrieval benchmarks by solving the quadratic attention scaling problem, with a 50-million-token window planned next. If the approach holds, it decouples long-context capability from the compute cost explosion that has made extended-context models prohibitively expensive.

items.title: In Search of Wasted Bits: How Much Information Do LLM Weights Carry?
items.summary: Analyzes the information-theoretic density of LLM weights to expose the real inference bottleneck: data movement, not computation, with compute units sitting idle while the memory bus can't feed them fast enough. Quantization helps bandwidth but doesn't trade memory for compute—the deeper fix is architectural transformation of memory into compute.

items.title: How to Scale Your Model (JAX ML Scaling Book)
items.summary: A comprehensive open book covering the science of scaling language models: how TPUs and GPUs work, how LLMs parallelize across hardware during training and inference, and how to answer questions about training cost and memory requirements at massive scale. Treats scaling as an engineering discipline with verifiable parameters rather than an empirical guessing game.

items.title: Google Rethinks Hallucinations Through Uncertainty
items.summary: Reframes LLM hallucinations as failures to express uncertainty rather than gaps in knowledge, proposing 'faithful uncertainty' as the alignment mechanism that would make model confidence track actual reliability. This shifts the problem from fact-retrieval engineering to calibration engineering—a meaningful change in how safety and interpretability teams approach hallucination mitigation.

items.title: The Case for Strategic Illegibility
items.summary: Making your company fully legible to AI systems risks commoditizing your own moat—founders should build a human-facing company and a parallel digital twin for AI agents, while deliberately keeping the weird, specific, hard-to-copy elements opaque. The highest-value things to protect from AI legibility are the tacit knowledge and contextual judgment that make a company genuinely differentiated. candidate-school: moat-illegibility — Strategic opacity to AI systems as a competitive defense; treating AI-unreadability of core workflows as an intentional moat.

items.title: The Agent Integration Tax Is Now the Biggest Market in Enterprise AI
items.summary: Coordination across enterprise systems—not model intelligence—is the dominant bottleneck to AI productivity, with Salesforce estimating that copying context across Slack, Salesforce, Workspace, and ServiceNow costs the average employee two hours per day. The emerging opportunity is governed agent registries with audit trails, not smarter models.

items.title: Don't Become a Wrapper
items.summary: Engineers who use agents to generate work without doing any reasoning themselves become as replaceable as the wrappers they ship—the risk is that if anyone with the same agent can produce identical outputs, individual skill becomes invisible and defensibility collapses. The antidote is maintaining genuine decision-making: understanding what to build, not just using tools that build it.

items.title: OpenAI Flips the Script
items.summary: OpenAI's Codex went from trailing Claude Code to pulling ahead in coding agent functionality within months, with GPT-5.5 paired with a faster desktop app enabling full migration of complex projects; explores what it means when tool leadership flips this quickly. The piece also frames 'permission to skip model-migration anxiety'—a practitioner response to the exhausting pace of new releases.

items.title: Silicon Valley Gets Serious About Services
items.summary: AI news digest documenting the accelerating shift of Silicon Valley toward services-as-software, with labs and AI-native startups pivoting to sell outcomes rather than tools and the first wave of AI-native service businesses showing SaaS-like margins. Covers the Agent Labs thesis and evidence that enterprise AI value is concentrating in outcome-delivery rather than model access.

items.title: The Impact of AI on Software Engineers in 2026: Key Trends
items.summary: Survey of 900+ software engineers finds that AI amplifies pre-existing patterns—strong engineers become dramatically more productive while weaker ones are exposed, and costs are rising faster than budgets with 30% already hitting token limits. The engineer and manager roles are converging as engineers orchestrate more and managers become more hands-on with code.

items.title: GPT-5.5 Instant: Smarter, Clearer, and More Personalized
items.summary: OpenAI updated ChatGPT's default model with 52.5% fewer hallucinated claims on high-stakes prompts, 30% more concise responses, and enhanced personalization from past chats, files, and connected Gmail for Plus and Pro users. The update positions GPT-5.5 Instant as a direct capability response to competition from Claude and Gemini in the default-model market.

items.title: Apple Plans Multi-Model AI in iOS 27
items.summary: Apple is making iOS 27 model-agnostic, allowing users to select from multiple third-party AI providers to power Siri and writing features rather than being locked to OpenAI. This represents a strategic pivot from exclusive partnership to AI marketplace, with major implications for how frontier models reach mainstream users.

items.title: Meta Plans Advanced Agentic AI Assistant Powered by Muse Spark
items.summary: Meta is building a highly personalized agentic AI assistant powered by the Muse Spark model that connects hardware and software tools and learns from data with less human intervention than a chatbot, targeting a launch before Q4 2026. Positions Meta as a serious competitor in the personal-agent market alongside Apple Intelligence and Google Gemini.

items.title: AI2 Released MolmoAct 2: Action Reasoning for Real-World Robots
items.summary: MolmoAct 2 is an upgraded open action-reasoning model that improves real-world robot task performance and is paired with a large open bimanual manipulation dataset, advancing physical AI toward general-purpose robotic agents. The open release strategy challenges proprietary physical AI labs by making state-of-the-art robot reasoning freely available.

items.title: Agents for Financial Services: Anthropic's 10 Ready-to-Run Templates
items.summary: Anthropic released 10 production-ready agent templates for the most time-consuming financial services work—pitchbooks, KYC screening, month-end close—as a direct play to convert services budgets into software spending. Signals Anthropic's move from model provider to services-as-software enabler in regulated industries.

items.title: New in Claude Managed Agents: Dreaming, Outcomes, and Multiagent Orchestration
items.summary: Anthropic's Managed Agents platform adds three self-improvement features: "dreaming" (reviewing past sessions to extract patterns), "outcomes" (agents self-correct toward a success rubric), and multiagent orchestration (lead agents delegate to specialized sub-agents). Completion rates rose sixfold in Harvey testing — these features push agents toward continuous self-improvement loops without human re-prompting.

items.title: Anthropic Partners with Blackstone, Hellman & Friedman, and Goldman Sachs to Launch Enterprise AI Services Firm
items.summary: Anthropic has joined Blackstone, Hellman & Friedman, and Goldman Sachs to create a new AI-native services firm that deploys Claude across enterprise operations — directly embodying the services-as-software thesis. This move blurs the line between model API provider and consulting firm, positioning Anthropic as a direct participant in the $6T global services market.

items.title: How AI Agent Memory Works
items.summary: A 28-minute deep dive into how memory systems in AI agents function, covering different architectures for carrying information forward across loops and why memory design is the key determinant of agent coherence across sessions. Foundational reading for anyone building or evaluating production agents.

items.title: World Models Can Change Everything
items.summary: The piece argues world models could advance AI from pattern recognition to genuine physical-world understanding, but the hard problem is obtaining diverse, high-quality real-world data at scale. Billions from LeCun and others are positioning world models as the next capability frontier beyond LLM-scale text training.

items.title: Open Weights Are Quietly Closing Up — And That's a Problem
items.summary: Escalating frontier training costs are driving model labs to release open-weight models under increasingly restrictive license conditions, threatening the competitive open-weights ecosystem that enabled privacy, flexibility, and cost efficiency. The author argues this has enormous economic implications as the gap between frontier proprietary and open models widens under tighter access terms.

items.title: Introducing Harvey's Legal Agent Benchmark (LAB)
items.summary: Harvey released an open-source benchmark for evaluating AI agent performance specifically on legal tasks, enabling systematic, reproducible measurement of agent reliability in high-stakes professional workflows. Represents the vertical-specific benchmarking trend that will shape how AI agents earn trust in regulated domains.

items.title: Google Is Not Building a Consultancy. It Is Writing a Licensing Agreement.
items.summary: Google is negotiating omnibus Gemini licensing deals with Blackstone, KKR, and EQT to give their entire PE portfolios AI access, betting that enterprise AI is a platform problem rather than a services delivery problem. The strategy trades consulting revenue for distribution speed — a contrast to Anthropic's simultaneous move to create a services firm.

items.title: All the Demons Hiding in Your AIs… Ranked
items.summary: A 40-minute taxonomy of LLM behavioral failure modes, including self-reinforcing stable states that resist suppression and spread into unrelated contexts — what the author calls demons. Essential alignment-adjacent reading for anyone deploying models in production where behavioral drift is a safety or reliability risk.

items.title: Everything We Announced at Stripe Sessions 2026
items.summary: Stripe announced 280+ new features at Sessions 2026 centered on agentic commerce: agent wallets, real-time billing infrastructure, and AI-powered checkout optimization that let AI agents transact and manage funds autonomously across fiat and stablecoin rails. Positions Stripe as financial infrastructure for the services-as-software economy where AI agents, not humans, initiate and settle financial flows.

items.title: Big Tech's Personal AI Agents Are Coming for the To-Do List
items.summary: Google's "Remy" inside Gemini and Meta's "Hatch" for Instagram are racing to build always-on AI assistants that anticipate needs and operate in the background — a platform-level shift from chatbots to deeply integrated personal agents. The push follows OpenClaw's viral rise exposing massive consumer demand for agentic AI while also revealing the limitations of standalone tools.

items.title: OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5 Instant as New ChatGPT Default
items.summary: OpenAI released GPT-5.5 Instant as the default ChatGPT model, with improved factual accuracy, reduced hallucinations, stronger personalization based on user context, and the ability to use saved memories and connected Gmail in responses. Updates the model serving the largest consumer AI audience, making reliability and personalization the competitive differentiators at the frontier.

items.title: Claude Code Is Not Making Your Product Better
items.summary: At the frontier, AI token spend doesn't reliably produce economic value — the bottleneck is tastemakers who know what to delete, compress, and refuse. The essay argues AI lowers the floor on generic product creation but doesn't help the highest-level artisans create better products, making taste and judgment more valuable, not less.

items.title: How to Work and Compound with AI
items.summary: Practical principles for compounding gains with AI as a collaborator: provide rich context, encode taste as reusable config, delegate bigger tasks end-to-end, and close the loop so every artifact becomes context for the next session. Each correction reduces future errors — treating AI workflow as a compounding system rather than a one-shot tool.

items.title: Cognitive Surrender
items.summary: Addy Osmani distinguishes "cognitive offloading" (delegating to AI while owning the answer) from "cognitive surrender" (borrowing AI confidence as your own without checking). Engineers drift between the two without noticing — this is the central capability-atrophy risk of heavy AI tool use in production.

items.title: Designing the AI-Native Engineering Organization
items.summary: A panel from Microsoft, 1Password, and Atlassian on how engineering organizations are restructuring for AI — covering how team size, review processes, and manager roles change when AI handles execution. Addresses whether the right response to AI productivity gains is fewer engineers, different engineers, or a fundamentally different org topology.

items.title: Programming in 2026: Excitement, Dread, and the Coming Wave
items.summary: A long practitioner essay about what it feels like to be a software engineer in 2026, when a major part of the job is "communicating with an alien technology we don't and can't fully understand." Captures the emotional and epistemological shift in programming as AI moves from tool to primary actor.

items.title: Vibe Coding and Agentic Engineering Are Getting Closer Than I'd Like
items.summary: Simon Willison reflects on realizing he can no longer clearly distinguish between vibe coding and agentic engineering in his own daily work — the two modes have converged. A practitioner observation from a key voice that the distinction between intentional and exploratory AI-assisted coding has collapsed in practice.

items.title: Live Blog: Code w/ Claude 2026
items.summary: Simon Willison's live blog of Anthropic's Code w/ Claude 2026 developer conference, covering keynote sessions, the Managed Agents announcements, and practitioner reactions in real time. Primary source coverage of the most significant developer AI event of May 2026.

items.title: ProgramBench
items.summary: ProgramBench challenges agents to recreate software executables from scratch using only documentation and experimentation — no source code, no decompilation — with 248,000 behavioral tests across 200 tasks ranging from terminal utilities to compilers. Sets a new bar for evaluating genuine software architecture capability in autonomous coding agents.

items.title: vLLM V0 to V1: Correctness Before Corrections in RL
items.summary: The vLLM V1 migration revealed subtle inference discrepancies — in logprob computation, prefix caching defaults, and fp32 precision — that were silently corrupting RL training signals and creating false objective improvements. Demonstrates that getting RL training right requires engineering correctness at the inference layer, not just the model or reward objective.

items.title: The Wrapper and the Code
items.summary: Apple rejected Replit's iOS app updates because its client previewed AI-generated apps inline — making the reviewed binary a container for unlimited unreviewed apps, an App Store policy violation. Highlights how platform gatekeeping creates structural friction for AI-native coding tools that generate runnable code as their core output.

items.title: The Culture of AI Engineering
items.summary: A framework for aligning humans, AI agents, and hybrid teams around a shared engineering vision, arguing that culture — not tooling — is the bottleneck for AI-native orgs. Essential read on what it means to manage engineering when agents do the implementation.

items.title: Inside Anthropic's 2026 Developer Conference
items.summary: First-hand account from Anthropic's developer conference covering what the new Managed Agents features (dreaming, outcomes, multiagent orchestration) look like in production. Documents Claude's shift toward genuinely self-improving agentic systems.

items.title: Codex Now Works Directly in Chrome on macOS and Windows
items.summary: OpenAI's Codex can now work in parallel across Chrome tabs in the background, handling structured browser workflows like data entry, Salesforce, and internal tools without taking over the session. A key step toward ambient agentic browsing where coding agents operate inside the browser itself.

items.title: OpenAI Releases Three Realtime Audio Models: GPT-Realtime-2, -Translate, -Whisper
items.summary: OpenAI releases GPT-Realtime-2 (GPT-5-class voice reasoning with 128K context), GPT-Realtime-Translate (70+ input languages, 13 output), and GPT-Realtime-Whisper (streaming transcription) as the Realtime API exits beta. Marks voice as a first-class modality for agentic applications.

items.title: Improving Token Efficiency in GitHub Agentic Workflows
items.summary: GitHub details how costs accumulate invisibly in automated agentic jobs and shares the instrumentation and optimizations its team applied to systematically reduce token usage. A practical engineering guide on the hidden economics of running agents at scale.

items.title: The Six-Hour Codex Run That Survived a Five-Hour Pause
items.summary: Deep dive into Codex's /goal feature — persistent goal states that survive terminal restarts, sleeps, and multi-hour interruptions by injecting a developer message on resume. Shows how long-horizon autonomous coding sessions are becoming a practical reality.

items.title: Good QC for RL Data
items.summary: Argues that RL data quality control needs a much higher bar, with most vendors failing multiple QC gates simultaneously against the performance-cost-latency Pareto curve. A practitioner view on why bad RL data quality will create serious problems for labs this year.

items.title: AlphaEvolve: How Our Gemini-Powered Coding Agent Is Scaling Impact Across Fields
items.summary: Google DeepMind shares how AlphaEvolve has been extended from mathematics and CS to explaining physics and accelerating scientific discovery across fields. Demonstrates the expanding scope of autonomous research agents from competitive benchmarks to real-world science.

items.title: Natural Language Autoencoders
items.summary: Anthropic introduces Natural Language Autoencoders to translate model activations into human-readable text for alignment auditing, including detecting hidden motivations and safety concerns in AI behavior. Despite hallucination and cost limitations, NLAs advance mechanistic interpretability toward production-ready auditing tools.

items.title: Notes from Inside China's AI Labs
items.summary: Dispatch from inside Chinese AI research culture: scientists do non-glamorous improvement work willingly, the ecosystem feels collaborative rather than tribal, and labs are less distracted by the business side. A substantive challenge to US assumptions about the China-US AI race.

items.title: Long AI Short AGI
items.summary: Silicon Valley's AGI-as-scarce-resource narrative is undermined by rapid model commoditization: intelligence is following the same curve as compute, bandwidth, and storage. The real winners won't own the best models but will own customer relationships and proprietary data — much like past tech giants.

items.title: Perplexity's Personal Computer Is Now Available to All Mac Users
items.summary: Perplexity opens its on-device AI agent to all Mac Pro and Enterprise users, giving AI access to local files, native apps, 400+ connectors, and the web through a persistent always-on process. Signals the shift from AI as chat interface to AI as operating-system layer.

items.title: Is My Company AI Pilled?
items.summary: OnlyCFO maps organizational AI maturity from basic prompt usage to full process automation, arguing that AI adoption success hinges on culture, hackathons, and sustained tooling investment rather than individual usage. A practitioner framework for AI-native org design.

items.title: The AI Job Apocalypse Is a Complete Fantasy
items.summary: Draws on Excel-era data (1M bookkeeping jobs lost, 1.5M analysts created), Atlanta Fed surveys (90%+ of firms saw zero AI headcount impact over three years), and earnings call analysis (8:1 augmentation over substitution) to dismantle the AI-kills-jobs narrative. Essential empirical counter-evidence.

items.title: AI Applications Have Two Growth Curves
items.summary: The fast-growth trajectory of companies like Cursor is not the only or most valuable curve for AI applications — the most durable businesses will grow slowly by solving problems competitors cannot copy in a quarter. Challenges treating Cursor-style growth as the universal template for AI investment.

items.title: The Best AI-Native GTM Plays You're Not Running
items.summary: Maps the evolution of AI-native go-to-market strategies toward continuous monitoring and dynamic responses, with AI agents autonomously managing multi-step sales workflows and signal-based engagement replacing calendar-based outreach. A practical guide to AI-agent-driven growth.

items.title: Behind the Scenes: Hardening Firefox with Claude Mythos Preview
items.summary: Mozilla used Claude Mythos Preview to identify and fix an unprecedented number of latent security bugs in Firefox, documenting the approach and advice for other projects. A detailed practitioner account of using AI agents for large-scale security hardening of a major open-source codebase.

items.title: Cloudflare Says AI Made 1,100 Jobs Obsolete, Even as Revenue Hit a Record High
items.summary: Cloudflare cut 20% of its workforce (1,100 people) amid record revenue and a 600% increase in AI agent usage in three months, explicitly restructuring around an agentic-AI-first operating model. The most prominent example to date of a tech company reorganizing headcount explicitly because agents replaced functions.

items.title: The Agent Principal-Agent Problem
items.summary: Contributors submitting agent-generated slop PRs create an unmanageable reviewing burden — reviewers would be more productive prompting agents themselves rather than reviewing agent-authored submissions. A sharp thesis on the economic misalignment when agents work for contributors rather than for reviewers.

items.title: The Problem with Mathematically Proven Claims About LLMs
items.summary: Systems keep improving while theorems keep arriving to explain why they cannot, because proofs and benchmarks are typically about different things. A useful corrective on the misuse of mathematical formalism to make confident claims about the limits of LLMs.

items.title: The April Every AI Plan Broke
items.summary: Documents how flat-rate AI subscription pricing breaks down when product capabilities and usage patterns evolve faster than pricing structures can adapt. Shows the SaaS model fracturing under variable compute costs per AI session.

items.title: Anthropic Releases Agents for Financial Services
items.summary: Anthropic launched ten finance-focused AI agent templates for pitchbook generation, KYC review, month-end close, valuation review, and financial modeling — plus Microsoft 365 add-ins and MCP integrations. Marks Claude's serious push into institutional finance workflows as services-as-software.

items.title: Anthropic Hit a $30 Billion Revenue Run Rate After an 80x Growth Quarter
items.summary: Anthropic's revenue grew 80-fold in Q1 on an annualized basis — far exceeding its own 10x plan — pushing it to a $30 billion run rate and requiring a deal to rent SpaceX's Colossus 1 data center. The growth rate suggests AI adoption is moving faster than any prior technology S-curve.

items.title: DeepSeek Eyes $50 Billion Valuation as China's AI Race Intensifies
items.summary: DeepSeek is in talks to close its first external fundraising at a $50 billion valuation, backed by China's National AI Industry Investment Fund — the strongest state signal yet of DeepSeek's role in China's AI strategy as a hedge against US export controls.

items.title: The Culture of AI Engineering
items.summary: Noah Brier presents a framework using Stewart Brand's pace layers for coordinating humans and AI agents to build the same shared vision, pushing back on the software factory metaphor. It matters because it is the first rigorous cultural framework for managing the coordination problems created by mixed human-agent teams.

items.title: Notes on the xAI/Anthropic Data Center Deal
items.summary: Simon Willison analyzes the unusual arrangement of Anthropic using SpaceX/xAI's Colossus data center at full capacity, despite xAI/Grok being a direct competitor. It signals how compute access has become so critical that even competing labs may share infrastructure, with implications for how AI capability scales.

items.title: Sticks, Stones, and Light Speed
items.summary: AI is reshaping the workforce by eliminating middle-layer coordination roles and elevating the importance of judgment and human connection at the edges. Argues that the durable human roles in an AI-transformed economy are those requiring genuine taste, accountability, and relational trust.

items.title: AI Work Is Splitting in Two
items.summary: AI work is bifurcating into two modes — real-time desktop collaboration and long-running delegated agents — and the emerging meta-skill is correctly routing tasks between them. Covers Anthropic Managed Agents, ChatGPT as project manager, and argues the allocation economy thesis only captured half the picture.

items.title: Contributor Poker and AI
items.summary: Open-source maintainers bet on contributors, not individual PRs — a trust model LLM-generated code breaks because it consumes code-review budget without building trusted human contributors. Articulates the clearest principled case for AI contribution bans, using Zig and Bun as live examples.

items.title: Anthropic-SpaceX compute deal shows how tokens are taking over the economy
items.summary: Argues that the Anthropic/SpaceX Colossus deal reveals how token consumption has become the new economic unit: harness engineering triggered an agentic explosion that outpaced Anthropic's compute supply, forcing a deal with Musk. Goldman Sachs estimates .6T in compute/data center/power infrastructure deployment through 2031.

items.title: Aaron Levie on X: unsentimental agent architecture
items.summary: Box CEO Aaron Levie argues that building production AI agents requires being ruthlessly unsentimental about architecture: as models improve they absorb scaffolding you built, and clinging to it actively hurts performance. A practitioner-level thesis on why agentic systems require continuous architectural rethinking rather than incremental additions.

items.title: Karpathy Declares Vibe Coding Obsolete, Introduces Agentic Engineering at Sequoia AI Ascent 2026
items.summary: Coverage of Karpathy's AI Ascent 2026 talk where he coined "agentic engineering" as the successor to vibe coding: vibe coding accepts what comes back, agentic engineering designs the system and uses AI to accelerate a plan you have already reasoned through. He also described his personal coding workflow shifting from 80% manual in November 2025 to 80% agent-directed by December — framing it as a threshold crossed, not a gradient.
